responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1990 5 1 92-92 A Politics and Planning in the National Health Service By Neil Small Milton Keynes: Open University Press £10.95. Sandra Grant
responseSet.title: Politics and Planning in the National Health Service By Neil Small Milton Keynes: Open University Press £10.95.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Politics and Planning in the National Health Service By Neil Small Milton Keynes: Open University Press £10.95.

Sandra Grant

Sandra Grant

Sandra Grant  

Neil Small guides the reader through the labyrinthian complexities of the structure and management of the National Health Service and the powerful political influences that determine it. He covers the major changes over the years between 1983 and 1989, from the intervention of Griffiths to the radical reforms in the White Paper, Working for Patients. The somewhat daunting account of a succession of reports, reviews and Health Service circulars is lightened by the inclusion of contemporary media reports recording personal views of the main protagonists. Small is particularly astute about the endless conflicting and contradictory analysis over numbers. It becomes pine-chillingly clear how the way figures are presented can change a cut in resources into an alleged growth. This is a well-researched book that remains remarkably readable despite the depth of information. The only area somewhat difficult to digest for the reader who comes from a clinical background is Small's rather terse descriptions of the work of theorists in management and sociology. I strongly recommend this book for anyone seriously concerned about the current crisis in the National Health Service.

92

responseSet.title: An English Freud?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An English Freud?

Malcolm Pines

Malcolm Pines

88 Montague Mansions
London W1H 1LF

Malcolm Pines  

This paper considers the cultural and academic climate in late-nineteenth-century Victorian culture and compares it with that of Austria and the rest of Europe. In many ways London was more isolated, more peripheral than Vienna. There were foreign influences, certainly, but they were not strong enough at first to shift the materialist concepts of the mind which were so prominent at that time. It was the great neurologists such as Hughlings Jackson who dominated the scene, and in this they were influential on Freud himself, but the climate was essentially opposed to the thinking that allowed psychoanalysis to flourish in Europe. However, despite this, the great pioneers of psychoanalysis, especially Ernest Jones, as well as the psychoanalytic psychotherapists interested in applying psychoanalytic principles more widely, did gradually turn the tide of opinion. Not an English Freud, but many institutions and individuals that we can be quite proud of.

responseSet.title: Obituary John Bowlby (27 February 1907-2 September 1990)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary John Bowlby (27 February 1907-2 September 1990)

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell  

I am pleased to acknowledge my appreciation of John Bowlby, his work and the man. I am indebted to Pearl King for some of the history.

John Bowlby became a member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society in November 1939, with Paula Heimann and Ronald Fairbairn. Already his interests were focussing: he wrote about the role of environmental factors in the development of neuroses and children's psychological difficulties. He had studied medicine at Cambridge University and worked at University College Hospital before becoming a psychiatrist at the Maudsley Hospital. He then worked in the Child Guidance Training Centre, then known as the London Child Guidance Clinic, from 1937 to 1940.

During the 1939-1945 war John Bowlby was on the War Office Selection Board. Alongside this he was Secretary of the Medical Committee of the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, with Adrian Stephens as Chairman. They produced important documents: a Training Scheme in relation to the Diploma in Psychological Medicine course, to take place at the Institute of psycho-Analysis; a Memorandum on the Teaching of Psychiatry; and a Memorandum on the Effects of a National Health Service on the Future of Psycho-Analysis.

After the war, following the ‘Controversial Discussions' (theoretical discussions between the Kleinian and Anna Freudian groups), the Institute's Training Committee was reorganised and John Bowlby became Secretary. There were objections to his holding that position since Bowlby was not a Training Analyst; but his talents as a good organiser carried him forward. He was involved in the careful negotiations that took place to instigate new training arrangements that acknowledged differences between the theoretical groups but that enabled the Society to stay together rather than split into smaller fragments. He produced the first training prospectus and generally improved the structure, organisation and professionalism of the training.

John Bowlby continued to be active in the British Psycho-Analytical Society, but more on the periphery. For example, in 1957 he proposed a Committee of Professional Practice to deal with concerns around the employment of psychoanalysts in private practice, the National Health Service, Universities and Research Establishments, and to promote communication. After a brief life this committee was disbanded. His concern continued, however, with his establishment of a Benevolent Fund. He became Chairman of the Board and Council (a committee on executive and staffing structure) where he did an enormous amount of work.

responseSet.title: Comment by David Bell on Tom Main's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comment by David Bell on Tom Main's Paper

D Bell

D Bell

Lower Flat 2 St Andrew's Grove, London N16 5NE

D Bell  

About a week before he died, I wrote to Tom Main, letting him know how important this paper, ‘Knowledge, Learning and Freedom from Thought’, had been for me. I am pleased to be involved in its publication here so that it can reach a wider readership. Apparently it was written on a plane during an attack of dysentery and Main thought it not well written, a view I do not share! I first came across the paper nearly three years ago, shortly after my arrival at the Cassel, when it was presented at a journal club by a senior registrar (Toby Thompson, to whom I remain indebted). Since then I have found myself referring to it in various different contexts. It is a paper of relevance not only to work in institutions but to psychoanalysis in general. It also raises some very important issues concerning the training of psychoanalysts, which I will return to later.

74

responseSet.title: The Therapist's Pregnancy: Evocative Intrusion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Therapist's Pregnancy: Evocative Intrusion**

Abigail Wedderkopp

Abigail Wedderkopp

Mrs A Wedderkopp Keepers Combe, Great Wood Over Stowey, Bridgwater, Somerset

Abigail Wedderkopp  

This paper is concerned with addressing the complex impact of the therapist's pregnancy on the analytic treatment process. The pregnancy is seen as intensifying the transference and countertransference material so that the therapeutic work becomes more strenuous as well as more fluid, with the potential for significant therapeutic change. The paper is divided into two parts. The first section attempts to conceptualise the meaning of the experience for the therapist and patient within the framework of psychoanalytic theory. Problems of technique and management resulting from the pregnancy are examined. One of the issues considered is the manner and timing of the patient's recognition of the pregnancy. The second section of the paper contains extensive clinical material illustrating the enriched transference and countertransference processes, and the access these allow to psychotic pockets of the personality, hitherto largely unavailable for exploration.

responseSet.documentRef: Bichard, S. H. (1990) Learning Disorders. An Integration of Neuropsychological and Psychoanalytic Considerations by Arden Rothstein, Lawrence Benjamin, Melvin Crosby and Katie Eisenstadt Madison: Int. Univ. Press 1988. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:87-91
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Bichard, S. H. (1990) Learning Disorders. An Integration of Neuropsychological and Psychoanalytic Considerations by Arden Rothstein, Lawrence Benjamin, Melvin Crosby and Katie Eisenstadt Madison: Int. Univ. Press 1988. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:87-91
responseSet.documentRefXML: Bichard, S. H. (1990) Learning Disorders. An Integration of Neuropsychological and Psychoanalytic Considerations by Arden Rothstein, Lawrence Benjamin, Melvin Crosby and Katie Eisenstadt Madison: Int. Univ. Press 1988. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:87-91
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1990 5 1 87-91 Learning Disorders. An Integration of Neuropsychological and Psychoanalytic Considerations by Arden Rothstein, Lawrence Benjamin, Melvin Crosby and Katie Eisenstadt Madison: Int. Univ. Press 1988 Sheila H Bichard
responseSet.title: Learning Disorders. An Integration of Neuropsychological and Psychoanalytic Considerations by Arden Rothstein, Lawrence Benjamin, Melvin Crosby and Katie Eisenstadt Madison: Int. Univ. Press 1988
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Learning Disorders. An Integration of Neuropsychological and Psychoanalytic Considerations by Arden Rothstein, Lawrence Benjamin, Melvin Crosby and Katie Eisenstadt Madison: Int. Univ. Press1988

Sheila Bichard

Sheila Bichard

Sheila Bichard  

Historically, the field of learning disability has been divided into those who see the problem primarily as an organic syndrome, often focusing on the aetiology of the condition, and those who locate it in the realm of education, focusing on remedial techniques. Alongside this debate there have been few contributions recently which relate to psychodynamic interpretations of learning disorders, and little or no attempt to integrate the various approaches.

The authors begin by commenting on these dichotomies. They are particularly concerned with putting forward an integrative perspective, because they feel that children and adolescents with learning disorders will best be served by this approach. The book is written primarily for those already familiar with psychoanalytic concepts and therefore focuses more heavily on neuropsychological factors, hoping to acquaint readers with unfamiliar parameters and controversies, and to foster better communications with analysts who work in this field.

The theoretical foundation on which the authors base their clinical work is the developmental framework, or interrelationship of physiological and psychological development. There is a very interesting section which deals with the impact of the child's neuropsychological development on developing ego functions. Specific neuropsychological dysfunctions are examined with reference to the development of object relations, and individual functions are looked at in the context of the child's experience of relating to the outside world.

Although the authors claim, in their attempt to define learning disorder, that the terminology is precise, there is much scope for overlap between categories. In fact, stating that learning disability is a learning disorder in children of normal intelligence, normal educational background and age-appropriate grade placement for chronological age, which is primarily based on unspecified but assumed neurocortical abnormalities or developmental lags, sounds suspiciously like the old definition of dyslexia. Spreen, in 1976, gave a do-it-yourself terminology generator with a list of qualifiers such as secondary, minimal, specific, developmental, a list of areas of involvement such as nervous, neurological, psychoneurologic, reading, and a list of problems such as deficit, dysfunction, handicap, impairment, and suggested that one could select any word from each of the columns, add a word from the second and another from the third, and it will mean about the same thing. It is disappointing to see that in the intervening years new terms such as cognitive power disorder, neurologically-based learning disorder, and neurogenic learning disorder, have sprung up. These terms lead one to assume that learning problems can be localised with a greater degree of accuracy than is suggested by research in the field.

responseSet.documentRef: Grant, S. (1990) Mutative Metaphors in Psychotherapy: the Aeolian Mode By Murray Cox and Alice Theilgaard London: Tavistock Social Science Paperback.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:91-92
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Grant, S. (1990) Mutative Metaphors in Psychotherapy: the Aeolian Mode By Murray Cox and Alice Theilgaard London: Tavistock Social Science Paperback.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:91-92

responseSet.title: Mutative Metaphors in Psychotherapy: the Aeolian Mode By Murray Cox and Alice Theilgaard London: Tavistock Social Science Paperback.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mutative Metaphors in Psychotherapy: the Aeolian Mode By Murray Cox and Alice Theilgaard London: Tavistock Social Science Paperback.

Sandra Grant

Sandra Grant

Sandra Grant  

This is an extremely exasperating book to read. There is a sense of something clinically important and relevant being described but it is disguised in a tortuous and wordy presentation which does it a considerable disservice. Nothing can be said without quoting someone else who said something similar. As the references draw on the whole field of literature and poetry, as well as psychoanalysis, they can be entertaining but ultimately side-tracking. To belabour the point about an excessive wordiness we also have to endure Acknowledgements, Fons et origo, Notation, Forward, Introduction and Prologue before starting the text.

The basic point being made (I think) concerns intuitive interpretations where the therapist uses his own free associations to the patient's material to make empathic contact. This is communicated to the patient via metaphor. The major strength of the book is in the clinical vignettes which provide beautiful examples of this process, especially with highly disturbed offender patients. At times, however, despite protestations to the contrary, one is left with a feeling that the prime qualification for a therapist is a capacity to recall verbatim the entire works of Shakespeare.

Perhaps my exasperation is predominantly based on envy: would that I were so cultured and knowledgeable as these authors. I suspect some people will find this book intoxicating and my criticism mealy-mouthed.

91

Between Freud and Klein: The Psychoanalytic Quest for Knowledge and Truth By Adam Limentani London: Free Association Books.

Marion Burgner

Marion Burgner

Marion Burgner  

Adam Limentani is a distinguished and well-known psychoanalyst of the Middle Group, the Independent Group, of the British Psycho-Analytical Society. He has held important offices in the British Society and in the International Psycho-Analytical Association; he is a training and supervising analyst of the British Society, a Fellow of the Royal College of Psychiatrists; and was for many years a consultant at the Portman Clinic in London, working with severely disturbed cases of sexual disorders and delinquency.

His philosophy of what the psychoanalyst and the psychotherapist are attempting to do is clearly stated in his Introduction:

responseSet.title: Thomas Forrest Main (25 February 1911-20 May 1990)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thomas Forrest Main (25 February 1911-20 May 1990)

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns  

‘Without the Cassel Hospital and the Tavistock Clinic there would be no psychoanalytical psychotherapy in the National Health Service today.’ —

Sir Denis Hill

Tom Main died at his home in Barnes on 29 May 1990, aged 79, without knowing the fate of the Cassel Hospital, the therapeutic community which as its Medical Director he began to develop in 1946, which he took into the National Health Service in good faith in 1948, and from which he retired in 1976. During his final illness, despite increasing weakness and in full knowledge of his prognosis, he had written for and encouraged the campaign to save the Hospital. Sadly, the news of its reprieve arrived only on the day of his funeral.

Mercifully he was free of pain until shortly before the end, and had time to review his achievements, to assess his own contribution, and to share his pride and his grief at the approach of death with his family, friends and colleagues. As his daughter I was grateful that we had been able to complete the work on the collection of papers published (The Ailment and other Psychoanalytic Essays, Free Association Books, 1989) shortly before his death, and that the book had been suitably launched and celebrated.

From a relatively impoverished background — the family had given up a comfortable life in South Africa and returned to Tyneside at the outbreak of the First World War — Tom Main was an outstanding scholar at the Royal Grammar School in Newcastle-upon-Tyne, winning scholarships there and to the Medical College of the University of Durham. His interests were in Science and in English, though all his school prizes were taken in the form of books of music — another passion. He played rugby for his school and for Durham Medicals, and enjoyed physical activity all his life, playing squash well into his fifties. Deeply influenced while a student and young doctor by James Spence, the paediatrician who first realised that babies survived in hospital more frequently if accompanied by their mothers, he also read Freud from the age of 15, and decided on a career in psychiatry at an early stage. After obtaining his MD at the age of 23, he discovered that it was possible to take a Dublin Diploma in Psychological Medicine in one day — Part One in the morning and Part Two after lunch.

responseSet.title: Knowledge, Learning, and Freedom from Thought
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Knowledge, Learning, and Freedom from Thought**

Tom Main

Tom Main

Tom Main  

This paper discusses the acquisition of knowledge by one generation from another, and the problems inherent in the process. The difficulties are seen as falling into three areas: (i) the difficulty of understanding the knowledge itself; (ii) the difficulty that a fact or a theory becomes an internal object subject to all the vicissitudes of object relations; and (iii) the difficulty of finding a training method that will enable the learner to assimilate knowledge and use it judiciously rather than to swallow it in an uncomprehending way. Examples are drawn from the history of ideas in general, and from the author's experience of therapeutic communities, particularly the Cassel Hospital. The danger of allowing a body of knowledge to become, in its passage from one person to another, a mere set of never-to-be-questioned beliefs is illustrated. The common frailties of both trainers and trainees are discussed and methods suggested for understanding and overcoming them.

responseSet.title: Sexual Abuse as a Theme in Group-Analytic Therapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexual Abuse as a Theme in Group-Analytic Therapy

Morris Nitsun

Morris Nitsun

Ilford, Essex, IG3 8XJ

Morris Nitsun  

The group-analytic approach, which emphasises the individual in a network of group relationships, is described with reference to the subject of sexual abuse in childhood. Three different National Health Service psychotherapy groups run by the author provide contrasting examples of how the theme, originating in an individual participant, was manifested in the dynamics of the group-as-a-whole. The therapeutic significance to the individual and the group is explored.

responseSet.title: The Value of Grandparents
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Value of Grandparents

Susanna Elmhirst FRCP FRCPsych

Susanna Elmhirst

1 Hollycroft Avenue London NW3 7QG

Susanna Elmhirst  

This paper is a slightly revised version of a paper presented on 26 October 1989 as one of the Tavistock Open Lectures. The role of grandparents is discussed and the value of a supportive presence, both externally and internally, is emphasised, especially at birth and at times of mental illness. This concept is extended to the maternal or ‘grand’-maternal role adopted by agencies attempting to improve the quality of mental care, and its value is also visible in the transference in psychotherapeutic work.
